title: Credential Compromise Revealed by Abnormal Card-Not-Present Transaction Ratio
narrative: Compromised card credentials are being exploited primarily through CNP channels while the legitimate cardholder continues normal card-present usage. The fraudster uses stolen card details for online purchases, creating an unusual dual-pattern where the card shows elevated CNP transaction counts (C13, C14) relative to historical baselines, while card-present activity (C11) remains suppressed or static. Individual transaction amounts and velocity appear normal, masking the compromise. The card's aggregated usage pattern reveals transactions from devices never paired with the card (D1=0), alongside elevated distinct product category counts (card1_distinct_products) indicating broader merchant-category-code diversity than the cardholder's established profile. Identity-verification features flag the mismatch between CNP concentration and the account's historical card-present preference, while device fingerprinting confirms first-time device-card associations across multiple recent transactions.
indicators: C13 > 24 (p90), elevated CNP transaction count in rolling window
C11 < 3 (median baseline), suppressed card-present transaction count
C14 > 7 (p90), high distinct email count indicating CNP channel diversity
D1 = 0 across recent transactions, indicating new device-card pairings
card1_distinct_products > p75 baseline, elevated MCC diversity for the card
C8 > 29 (p90), high address usage count suggesting multiple delivery destinations
V200 > p90, Vesta engineered feature flagging CNP-to-card-present ratio anomaly
indicator_explanations: Card shows unusually high count of online transactions in recent activity window
Card-present transactions are minimal or absent compared to historical cardholder behavior
Card used with many different email addresses across online checkout flows
Multiple recent transactions from devices that have never transacted with this card
Card purchasing across broader merchant categories than cardholder's typical pattern
Card linked to numerous different shipping addresses in aggregated view
Model feature detects abnormal shift from in-person to remote transaction preferences
summary: Stolen card credentials are being used for CNP transactions while legitimate card-present usage remains low, creating an unusual ratio mismatch. Device and aggregation features reveal first-time pairings and elevated merchant-category diversity inconsistent with cardholder history.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders maintain consistent transaction-channel preferences; a sudden concentration of CNP activity with suppressed card-present usage, combined with new device associations and elevated address/email/product diversity, indicates credential theft where the fraudster operates exclusively in remote channels while the cardholder's in-person behavior remains dormant or continues separately.
